content.problemStatement:
Duplicate rows in a DataFrame are rows that have the same values across all columns or a subset of columns. 

Methods for Identifying Duplicates
- Using duplicated() method: This method returns a boolean Series indicating whether each row is a duplicate of any of the previous rows
   - Note that all the values of the row are checked while declaring the row as a duplicate
   - The original row is not flagged as a duplicate. Only the next instance of the exact same values are flagged as duplicate
- Using duplicate_subset parameter: You can specify a subset of columns to consider when identifying duplicates.
   - Note that only the specific column value is considered when identifying duplicates

We have populated a dataframe in the IDE - go ahead and check how the above methods work.
- Note that only the last row is a duplicate row - hence this is the only value which is TRUE
- Note that based on Column 'A' - the values 2 and 3 are duplicate. Hence row index 2, 5 and 6 are flagged as duplicate
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
